items.title: How does one make sense of SF? We are living through the craziest computing revolution of the l…
items.content: How does one make sense of SF? We are living through the craziest computing revolution of the last 2 decades....and 95% of the value being created is within a 4-mile radius in SF (h/t @eladgil ) It's just hard to understand....why is the concentration so high? What is it about this place? There are good reasons that people will offer...good universities, great companies, talent networks, venture capital yada yada yada But all of that is hard to emotionally understand. I tend to try to answer th…

items.title: The arc of a LLM skeptic: 1. They are just auto-correct 2. They are just next-token predictors…
items.content: The arc of a LLM skeptic: 1. They are just auto-correct 2. They are just next-token predictors 3. They hallucinate 4. They can only be useful for the data they are trained on 5. They are only good for coding 6. They are only good for verifiable things Just admit it! LLMs are awesome.

items.title: Every day I find someone who manages to get LLMs to do something weird and interesting. I have…
items.content: Every day I find someone who manages to get LLMs to do something weird and interesting. I have to keep on reminding myself that we are in the super early innings of figuring out what we can build with this magical technology.

items.content: It's interesting...the Anthropic culture reminds me a lot of early Facebook....despite OpenAI having a lot more ex-Facebook people Similarities: --> People within the company are having a lot of fun --> There is a lot of naivete about the world --> There is a bottoms-up hacker culture --> There is insane "mission" alignment --> They might be taking themselves just a bit too seriously --> Winning solves a lot of things

items.title: The big difference between a true "agent" and simply running an LLM in a loop (or an event-base…
items.content: The big difference between a true "agent" and simply running an LLM in a loop (or an event-based trigger) is how you do intelligent long-horizon memory management. By far the most interesting aspect of the Claude Code architecture was their 3-tier memory architecture.

items.title: The implications of "free" software are truly hard to wrap my head around. You don't like an in…
items.content: The implications of "free" software are truly hard to wrap my head around. You don't like an interface? Just design and push a new one immediately. Performance is slow? Just re-factor the data layer with auto-performance optimization. Insane.

items.title: "First you shape the tools, then the tools shape you". At SPC, our entire team is now writing c…
items.content: "First you shape the tools, then the tools shape you". At SPC, our entire team is now writing code on a weekly basis. Two months ago, there were only 1-2 people writing code. This has been incredible on many levels but the most interesting one is how the tools are now shaping us as a team: - Everyone has a mindset towards automation and optimization. - Latencies for everything are lower. - People can focus on the more interesting parts of their roles. - The scope of everyone's ambition has expl…

items.title: A lot of the conversation circa 2009 was about making the web 2.0 read/write and malleable. We…
items.content: A lot of the conversation circa 2009 was about making the web 2.0 read/write and malleable. We are living through that today but for all software. What if all software could be forked, changed, re-mixed and customized?
